channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: How Quiet Oman Landed Itself in Trump’s Cross Hairs
item.description: As the Iran war drags on, Oman — a U.S. ally and mediator with Iran — has found itself at odds with the Trump administration and some of its own neighbors.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Fishing in Muscat, Oman, in February, before the war between the United States, Israel and Iran started.

item.title: The Roar of ‘Cease-Fire’ in a Once-Thriving City
item.description: The city of Nabatieh once spoke to the changing fortunes of Lebanon. Now, amid unending war, it speaks to the country’s worst fears.
item.dc:creator: Christina Goldbaum, Hwaida Saad and Daniel Berehulak
item.media:description: An empty road to Nabatieh, in southern Lebanon, last week.

item.title: Stray Drones Are Setting Off Alarms in Europe, and the U.S. Deports a World Cup Referee
item.description: Plus, why Americans are clamoring for European sunscreen.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart and Tariq Panja
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ukraine has been sending swarms of drones to hit Russian ports, oil terminals and other facilities on the Baltic Sea — but some have veered off course en route.

item.title: The Battle for the Senate Could Come Down to Hopes and Fears in Maine
item.description: Graham Platner’s primary victory in Maine sets up a high-stakes contest between an insurgent progressive with political baggage and a battle-tested but vulnerable Republican senator.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner will face Senator Susan Collins, a moderate Republican, in a general election in Maine that is seen as central to the fight for Senate control.

item.title: Takeaways From Tuesday’s Primary Elections in Maine and South Carolina
item.description: Graham Platner won the Democratic primary for Senate in Maine and quickly turned his populist message against Susan Collins, the Republican incumbent.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: Steve Hilton Wins Second Spot in California Governor Race, Beating Tom Steyer
item.description: Steve Hilton, a Republican endorsed by President Trump, will face Xavier Becerra, a Democrat, in the general election. Tom Steyer, another Democrat, will not advance.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:description: Xavier Becerra, left, and Steve Hilton.

item.title: Inside Trump’s White House, the Epstein Files Caused a Freakout
item.description: The president’s top advisers gathered in a series of Situation Room meetings as they struggled to contain a scandal engulfing Donald Trump himself.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan

item.title: The White House Panic Over the Epstein Files: Six Takeaways From the New York Times Investigation
item.description: Senior officials clashed in a series of meetings as they struggled to manage a crisis over the president’s refusal to release the documents.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.media:description: Protesters at the White House last July.

item.title: A Twist in Ukraine’s Drone Campaign Is ‘Really Hurting the Russians’
item.description: Midrange attacks, using upgraded drones that Ukraine produces in huge numbers, are causing fuel shortages and complicating troop rotations.
item.dc:creator: Marc Santora
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Soldiers preparing to launch drones at a targets in Russia from an undisclosed location in Ukraine last month.

item.title: Visiting a Soldier’s Funeral in Ukraine
item.description: The New York Times reporter Kim Barker visited a church in Lviv, Ukraine, that holds military funerals at the same time each day.
item.dc:creator: Kim Barker, Whitney Shefte, Michael Anthony Adams, Oleksandra Mykolyshyn, Sutton Raphael and Rebecca Suner

item.title: Dutch Authorities Block $115 Million Deal by a U.S. Tech Company
item.description: The Netherlands blocked a U.S. company from buying a Dutch firm that handles its national ID system, saying it would create a “threat to the public interest.”
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano and Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Laurens Van Putten/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Willemijn Aerdts, the Dutch minister for the digital economy and sovereignty, spoke to the news media last month after blocking the acquisition of Solvinity, a Dutch tech company, by the U.S. firm Kyndryl.

item.title: Blanche Was Once Seen as Tempering Trump’s Tactics. Now He’s All In.
item.description: The ascendancy of Todd Blanche shows how the practices that were initially deemed out of bounds even in President Trump’s Justice Department seem to be the order of the day.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, during a cabinet meeting at the White House in May.

item.title: Trump Says Bill Pulte Will Take Over as Intelligence Chief on June 19
item.description: The president also plans to keep Mr. Pulte as the head of a federal housing agency, heightening tension with Congress and complicating the renewal of a powerful spying authority.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.category: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:description: President Trump has suggested that Bill Pulte might use the temporary appointment as director of national intelligence as an opportunity to purge the office of perceived political opponents.

item.title: Trump’s Sharp Turn on China: Embracing It as a Peer Power
item.description: President Trump’s warming with Xi Jinping of China, a leader he admires, has ignited anxieties in Washington and across Asia.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.media:description: The U.S. and Chinese governments rolled out a new phrase, “constructive strategic stability,” during President Trump’s meeting in Beijing last month with the leader of China, Xi Jinping.

item.title: The Knicks Are Hogging the Spotlight. Spurs Fans Aren’t Impressed.
item.description: As New York basks in the attention of a long-awaited N.B.A. finals run, many San Antonians say the Spurs, and the city they represent, are being underestimated.
item.dc:creator: Edgar Sandoval
item.media:credit: Christopher Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Spurs fans at an outdoor watch party at The Pearl for Game 3 of the NBA Finals in San Antonio.

item.title: New York Is Buzzing With Watch Parties for the Knicks’ NBA Finals Games
item.description: In bodegas and parks, in bars and pizzerias, the unofficial watch party has taken over New York.
item.dc:creator: David Waldstein and The New York Times

item.title: For the NBA Finals, These Knicks Fans Show Out in Style
item.description: Fans want to support their team, but they’re not willing to squelch their sense of style.
item.dc:creator: Simbarashe Cha and Jacob Gallagher

item.title: SpaceX IPO: What You Need to Know
item.description: As the company prepares for its stock market debut, here’s what the initial public offering means for banks, Elon Musk and everyday investors.
item.dc:creator: Aruni Soni
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Shares of SpaceX are expected to start trading on Friday.

item.title: A Catholic Mayor Commissioned Statues of Saints. Residents Revolted.
item.description: The mayor of Quincy, Mass., is being sued over 10-foot statues of two Catholic saints that he wants to install in front of a new public safety complex.
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell

item.title: ‘Clumsy Diplomacy’: Inside the U.S.-Kenya Feud Over an Ebola Camp
item.description: Hundreds of Kenyans have marched through the streets to oppose a quarantine facility that would be reserved exclusively for American patients.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg and Eric Schmitt
item.media:description: Protesters confronting Kenyan police officers at a demonstration against a U.S. Ebola quarantine facility in Nanyuki on Tuesday.

item.title: Somali World Cup Referee Denied Entry to U.S. Returns Home to Hero’s Welcome
item.description: Omar Abdulkadir Artan, who is widely admired in his home country, would have been the first Somali to referee a World Cup game.
item.dc:creator: Hussein Mohamed
item.media:description: Omar Abdulkadir Artan, a soccer referee who had planned to officiate at the World Cup, arriving back in Mogadishu, Somalia, on Wednesday.

item.title: Argentina’s World Cup Sticker Albums Are a Low-Tech Craze Immune to the Digital Revolution
item.description: World Cup sticker albums have captivated children and adults in Argentina, in a collecting fever that seems immune to the digital revolution.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola, Lucía Cholakian Herrera and Anita Pouchard Serra
item.media:credit: Anita Pouchard Serra for The New York Times
item.media:description: People exchanging World Cup stickers in Buenos Aires last month.

item.title: How Maya Rudolph Became an ‘Absolute Menace’ in ‘Oh, Mary!’
item.description: For her Broadway debut, the comedian hired a trainer, read lines with her daughter and, when she needed it most, was saved by improv.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter and Thea Traff
item.media:description: Rudolph’s first thought said about stepping into the role, “was, how do I do what they did?” she said. “I remember saying, I can’t find my voice.”

item.title: Taking a Week to Count Votes Is Doing It Wrong
item.description: It is time for California to catch up to its own past.
item.dc:creator: The Editorial Board
item.media:credit: Illustration by Rebecca Chew/The New York Times

item.title: ‘A Mighty Form of Communion’: Three Writers on the World Cup’s Strange Magic
item.description: There’s ample cause for celebration and consternation alike.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Jelly-Schapiro, Juan Villoro and Andrew Potter

item.title: You Asked
item.description: We’re answering reader questions.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Calls a Technical Foul on Trump’s Apparent Game 3 Nap
item.description: The late night host called Trump “the first sitting president to shut down a major United States city so he could take a nap in front of a sold-out crowd at the N.B.A. finals.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Pope Leo Is Blessing the Sagrada Familia in Barcelona. To Some, the Church Is a Curse.
item.description: The pontiff will offer a Mass in Antoni Gaudí’s iconic, unfinished basilica during his Spain trip. But for some Barcelona residents, completing the construction could mean knocking down their homes.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz
item.media:description: The basilica of Sagrada Familia in Barcelona, Spain, this month.
